query: anatomical origin of greater palatine artery compared to nasal septum

retrieved_docs.title: Septoplasty -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Blood Supply
retrieved_docs.content: The blood supply to the nasal septum travels via a collection of arteries derived from the internal and external carotid arteries. The internal carotid artery gives rise to the anterior and posterior ethmoidal arteries (via the ophthalmic artery), which supply the superior aspect of the septum. The external carotid gives rise to the facial and maxillary arteries, the terminal branches of which provide the remaining vascular supply; the facial artery branches form the superior labial artery, which supplies the anterior aspect of the nose. The maxillary artery branches form the greater palatine and sphenopalatine arteries, which supply the inferior and posterior septum. These five arteries (anterior and posterior ethmoid, superior labial, greater palatine, and sphenopalatine) anastomose anteroinferiorly at Little's area on the septum to form Kiesselbach's plexus, which is the most common site of epistaxis.

retrieved_docs.title: Sinus Endoscopic Surgery -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The septum is a rigid structure and consists of bony and cartilaginous parts. The septal cartilage forms the anterior part of the septum. The perpendicular plate of the ethmoid is found postero-superiorly, the vomer postero-inferiorly, and the crest of the maxillary and palatine bones form the base of the septum. The septum is covered by mucosa and has an extensive blood supply between the mucosa and the perichondrium. The arteries that supply the septum consist of the sphenopalatine artery, which is a branch of the maxillary artery; the anterior and posterior ethmoidal arteries originating from the ophthalmic artery; the septal branch of the superior labial artery deriving from the facial artery; and the septal branch of the ascending and more significant palatine arteries branching from the maxillary artery. Little's area is a vascular area where several arteries anastomose and form the Kiesselbach's plexus, a common area of anterior epistaxis.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sphenopalatine Artery -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The SPA branches into two major vessels, the septal artery and posterior lateral nasal artery, before exiting the sphenopalatine foramen. [6] The septal artery exits the sphenopalatine foramen, courses through the anteroinferior wall of the sphenoid sinus, and distributes on the nasal septum.  The posterior lateral nasal artery exits the sphenopalatine foramen, courses downward anteriorly to the posterior end of the middle turbinate along the lateral nasal wall, and runs inferiorly on the perpendicular plate of the palatine bone giving off branches to the inferior and middle turbinate. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nasal Cavity -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Maxillary Artery
retrieved_docs.content: Like the descending palatine artery, the sphenopalatine artery is a branch of the maxillary artery. It branches off of the maxillary artery near the pterygopalatine fossa where it then enters the lateral wall of the nasal cavity through the sphenopalatine foramen, located just posterior to the medial turbinate. The sphenopalatine artery then branches into the posterior lateral nasal branches and the posterior septal branch. The posterior lateral branches supply the middle and inferior turbinates, while the posterior septal branch supplies the posterior septum. [1] [2] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nasal Cavity -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Kiesselbach's Plexus (Little's Area)
retrieved_docs.content: Kiesselbach's plexus is a vascular anastomosis between the anterior ethmoid artery, superior labial artery, greater palatine artery, and the terminal branch of the posterior septal branch of the sphenopalatine artery. This vascular plexus is located in the anterior nasal septum and is the most common site of epistaxis. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nasal Cavity -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Maxillary Artery
retrieved_docs.content: The maxillary branches into the descending palatine artery that then travels through the pterygopalatine fossa down the palatine canal and then branches into the greater and lesser palatine arteries. The greater palatine artery then enters the greater palatine foramen on the posterior aspect of the palate before traversing the palate anteriorly to enter the nasal cavity via the incisive canal. It supplies the septum and the floor of the nasal cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Internal Maxillary Arteries -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Pharyngeal artery : enters the palatovaginal canal Greater palatine artery : enters the greater palatine foramen Gives rise to the lesser palatine branch Artery of the pterygoid canal Sphenopalatine artery : enters the sphenopalatine foramen Gives rise to the posterior lateral nasal arteries Gives rise to the posterior septal branches Posterior superior alveolar artery Infraorbital artery : enters inferior orbital fissure Gives rise to the anterior superior alveolar artery

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Internal Maxillary Arteries -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The pharyngeal artery supplies the posterior aspect of the roof of the nasal cavity, sphenoid sinus, and Eustachian tube. The greater palatine artery provides blood to the hard palate, and the lesser palatine artery gives supply to the soft palate. The sphenopalatine artery terminates into the posterior lateral nasal artery and posterior septal branches. The posterior lateral nasal artery supplies the lateral wall of the nasal cavity, and the posterior septal branches supply the nasal septum of which some branches anastomose with the greater palatine artery. The posterior superior alveolar artery supplies the lower mandible, molar, premolars, and surrounding gingiva. The infraorbital artery supplies the lower eyelid and upper lip. The anterior superior alveolar artery supplies the incisors and canines. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sphenopalatine Artery -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The sphenopalatine artery and its branches form an anastomotic region within the nasal cavity. Kiesselbach's plexus (Little's area) is the arterial anastomosis found in the anterior nasal septum formed by terminal branches of the internal carotid artery (ICA) and the external carotid artery (ECA). The Kiesselbach's plexus is formed by the anterior and posterior ethmoidal artery (branches of the ICA) and ECA branches such as the superior labial artery, greater palatine artery, and the posterior septal branch of the SPA. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sphenopalatine Artery -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: The sphenopalatine artery anatomy has been studied extensively by numerous researchers. Ninety-seven percent of these cadaveric studies have demonstrated at least two arterial branches at the plane of the sphenopalatine foramen. [7] These branches are the posterior lateral and septal branches, as discussed earlier. Although two to four branches off of the SPA is the most common arrangement, some studies have demonstrated as many as ten branches. [14] Once the sphenopalatine artery exits the sphenopalatine foramen, the more distal and posterolateral contributions have been shown to have greater variability. [15] Additionally, it has been observed that some portions of the artery may run anteriorly to the posterior maxillary wall.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nose -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The superior septum is supplied by the anterior and posterior ethmoidal arteries and the sphenopalatine artery. Terminal branches of the superior labial artery supply the anterior septum and the nasal floor. The anterior septum gets its blood supply by the superior labial, anterior ethmoidal, greater palatine and sphenopalatine arteries in a confluence called Kiesselbach’s plexus.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lateral Nasal Artery -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The posterior lateral nasal artery is a branch of the sphenopalatine artery, itself a branch of the internal maxillary division of the external carotid artery, that arises along with the posterior septal artery at a bifurcation that occurs just before the arteries' entrance into the nasal cavity via the sphenopalatine foramen. [6] The posterior lateral nasal artery perfuses the posterior aspect of the lateral nasal wall. It gives off branches to the middle and inferior turbinates, while the posterior septal artery feeds the superior turbinate. The posterior lateral nasal artery then anastomoses with the posterior ethmoidal artery superiorly and the anterior lateral nasal artery via the inferior turbinate. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Ascending Palatine Artery -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The ascending palatine artery branches off of the facial artery and anastomoses with the tonsillar artery, ascending pharyngeal artery, and descending palatine artery. The artery goes on to supply the eustachian tube, palatine tonsils, and the soft palate; palatoglossus, palatopharyngeus, tensor veli palatini, musculus uvulae, and the levator veli palatini. [1] [2] During surgery, the blood supply to the soft palate may be cut off due to damage to the ascending palatine artery; this is especially so in patients with absent anterior branches and posterior branches that stop close to the uvula. [1] The anterior branch has a mean diameter of 0.73 +/- 0.09 mm, as where the posterior branch measures 0.48 +/- 0.08 mm in diameter. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Epistaxis Nasal Pack -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most commonly implicated arterial blood supply in anterior epistaxis is the Kiesselbach plexus in Little’s area (Image 1). [2] This plexus is located on the anteroinferior nasal septum, just above the vestibule, and is readily visible during anterior rhinoscopy (Image 2). The Kiesselbach plexus comprises the anterior ethmoidal branch of the ophthalmic artery, the sphenopalatine branch of the maxillary artery, the superior labial branch of the facial artery, and the greater palatine branch of the maxillary artery. The ethmoidal arteries arise from the internal carotid system, while the sphenopalatine, greater palatine, and superior labial arteries are terminal branches of the external carotid system. These arteries supply the nasal mucosa and the underlying bone and cartilage, the latter via perichondrial microvasculature.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Ascending Palatine Artery -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The ascending palatine artery most commonly originates from the facial artery and runs alongside the pharynx and passes between the styloglossus and the stylopharyngeus muscles. Its location is with the levator veli palatini where it divides into two branches to supply the soft palate and palatine glands. It later anastomoses with the descending palatine artery; which is a branch of the maxillary artery.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasotracheal Intubation -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Respiratory Mucosa
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the high vascularity of the nasal cavity, minor trauma to any part of the tissue can cause bleeding (epistaxis). The anterior nasal septum is particularly susceptible to developing epistaxis due to the arterial plexus's superficial location. This confluence is known as Kiesselbach’s plexus and is supplied by branches of the anterior and posterior ethmoid, superior labial, sphenopalatine, and greater palatine arteries. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1807
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose encompass the specific intervention of control of epistaxis, which is classified as a type of nasal procedure. The control of epistaxis is performed directly on the nose (nos), indicating that the nose is the precise anatomical site targeted by this bleeding‑control technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior epistaxis is identified as a disorder of the nasal septum (اضطراب في الحاجز الأنفي), representing bleeding that originates from the front portion of the nasal cavity and is commonly associated with structural or mucosal abnormalities of the septum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576
retrieved_docs.content: Control of epistaxis is a medical procedure whose direct site of action is the nose (nos).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1978
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior epistaxis is classified as اضطراب في الحاجز الأنفي (a disorder of the nasal septum). This nasal‑septum disorder is mapped to the category “other upper respiratory disease,” which in turn is placed under the broader “nos” (not otherwise specified) parent classification. This hierarchical relationship situates anterior epistaxis within the larger framework of upper respiratory conditions, linking a specific type of nosebleed to a general respiratory disease group and ultimately to a nonspecific diagnostic category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are medical interventions that address nasal disorders (اِضطِراب أنفي). Within this category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of Procedure on Nose, indicating that managing nosebleeds is a subset of nasal procedural care. This relationship highlights that interventions aimed at controlling epistaxis are directly linked to the broader management of nasal disorders, emphasizing the role of targeted procedural treatment in the evaluation and care of patients with nasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 28
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior epistaxis is classified as a type of “اضطراب في الحاجز الأنفي” (nasal septum disorder). In the disease hierarchy, the label “other upper respiratory disease” is mapped to this same nasal septum disorder and is placed under the broader category “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (nasal disorders not classified elsewhere), which in turn is a parent of “اِضطِراب أنفي” (nasal disorder). The nasal septum itself—referred to in Czech as “nosní přepážka” and abbreviated as “nos”—is identified as the finding site for “other upper respiratory disease” and serves as the parent concept for that disease group. Surgical interventions that manipulate a displaced nasal septum have a direct procedure site on the “nosní přepážka” (nasal septum). These interventions are organized as a child of the category “NOSE AND NASOPHARYNX: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES,” which is itself a child of the broader activity “control of epistaxis.” The same operative category includes the child “عمليات الحاجز الأنفي” (nasal septum surgeries). “Procedures on Nose” are linked to “اِضطِراب أنفي” (nasal disorder) and are considered a broader class that encompasses the specific activity of controlling epistaxis. The “control of epistaxis” procedure targets the nasal septum (“nos”) as its direct procedure site and is focused on treating “الرُعاف” (epistaxis), which is also listed as a child of the “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” category. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how anterior epistaxis, nasal septum pathology, and related surgical procedures are organized within a hierarchical framework of upper respiratory and nasal disorder classifications, emphasizing the central role of the nasal septum in both diagnosis and therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 235
retrieved_docs.content: أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر also has الرُعاف as a child concept, reinforcing the connection between unspecified nasal disorders and bleeding from the nose. Overall, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical and functional network linking nasal‑septum disorders, their classification within upper‑respiratory disease taxonomy, and the corresponding diagnostic and therapeutic procedures—particularly those aimed at controlling epistaxis—thereby providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing nasal‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, indicating that these examinations focus on assessing the structure and function of the larynx (often in conjunction with the adjacent trachea) as part of otolaryngologic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 714
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose encompass the specific intervention known as control of epistaxis, indicating that control of epistaxis is a subtype of nasal procedures. The control of epistaxis procedure is performed directly on the nose (nos), targeting the anatomical site where bleeding occurs. Its primary clinical focus is the management of الرُعاف, the Arabic term for epistaxis (nosebleed). In the broader classification system, الرُعاف is listed as a child category under اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر, which refers to nasal disorders not classified elsewhere. Together, these relationships highlight that controlling epistaxis is a nose‑directed procedure, central to treating nosebleeds, and that epistaxis itself is part of a larger, miscellaneous group of nasal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1954
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx are categorized as a sub‑type of عمليات الأنف, indicating that they fall within the broader group of nose‑related surgical interventions. These diagnostic procedures are closely linked to منظار داخلي, an internal endoscopic technique that is commonly employed to visualize and assess the nasal cavity, oral cavity, and pharyngeal structures. Understanding that diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx belong to عمليات الأنف and utilize منظار داخلي helps clinicians select appropriate endoscopic tools for accurate evaluation and management of upper airway conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 994
retrieved_docs.content: The nose (nos) is classified as a component of “other upper respiratory disease,” which in turn is a subcategory of the broader group “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (unspecified nasal disorders) and is linked by an inverse‑isa relationship to “اِضطِراب أنفي” (nasal disorder). The nasal septum (nosní přepážka) is identified as a primary finding site for “other upper respiratory disease” and serves as the direct procedural site for the “Manipulation of displaced nasal septum.” This manipulation, as well as other “عمليات الحاجز الأنفي” (nasal septum operations), are organized under the category “NOSE AND NASOPHARYNX: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES.” That operative category is hierarchically connected to “control of epistaxis,” which is positioned as a parent concept for the operative group. “Control of epistaxis” directly targets the nose (nos), focuses on “الرُعاف” (epistaxis), and is a child of the unspecified nasal disorders group. Additionally, “Procedures on Nose” are related to “اِضطِراب أنفي” (nasal disorder) and have an inverse‑isa relationship with “control of epistaxis.” Together, these relationships delineate a structured hierarchy in which the nose and nasal septum are central anatomical sites for upper‑respiratory diseases, specific nasal disorders, and related surgical interventions, while epistaxis is recognized both as a distinct clinical focus and as a sub‑entity within the broader classification of unspecified nasal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 274
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior epistaxis is classified as a disorder of the nasal septum (اضطراب في الحاجز الأنفي), and this nasal septum disorder is also mapped to the broader category of other upper respiratory disease, linking anterior epistaxis to the wider group of upper respiratory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1141
retrieved_docs.content: The nose (nos) is classified as a component of “other upper respiratory disease,” which in turn falls under the broader category “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (nasal disorders not classified elsewhere). Within this hierarchy, “اِضطِراب أنفي” (nasal disorder) is a specific subclass of “other upper respiratory disease.” Procedures that are performed on the nose are linked to “اِضطِراب أنفي,” and one such procedure is the “control of epistaxis,” which is considered a type of nose‑related procedure (inverse_isa). The “control of epistaxis” is directly performed on the nose (direct_procedure_site_of nos) and its clinical focus is “الرُعاف” (nosebleed). Additionally, “الرُعاف” is listed as a child condition within the category “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر.” Together, these relationships illustrate how nasal anatomy, disorder classifications, and specific therapeutic interventions such as epistaxis control are interconnected within the framework of upper respiratory disease taxonomy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2834
retrieved_docs.content: The term جراحة (surgery) functions as a primary category that encompasses more specialized procedural types, including ultrazvuková chirurgie (ultrasound‑guided surgery) and البزل (aspiration), each of which is described as having جراحة as its parent. In addition, the concept clinically_associated_with is linked to جراحة and عمليات الأنف (nose surgeries), indicating that clinical associations are recognized between general surgical practice and specific nasal operative interventions. This hierarchical and relational structure highlights how broad surgical disciplines relate to both advanced technique‑specific procedures and particular anatomical applications, informing a comprehensive understanding of surgical taxonomy and its clinical relevance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx and are organized as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum. According to the indirect_procedure_site_of relationship, the larynx also serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. This dual classification highlights the larynx’s central role both as the primary anatomical focus of specific diagnostic interventions and as a secondary site affected by broader non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, emphasizing its importance in the overall assessment and management of respiratory tract conditions.
